title: The Larger Catechism and the Lord’s Supper, Part 5
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: The Larger Catechism and the Lord’s Supper

outline: Q.174: What is required of him that administereth the Lord’s Supper?
Q.175: What is the duty of Christians after they have received the Lord’s Supper?
Summary of the catechism’s full teaching on the Lord’s Supper
summary: Concluding (fifth) lesson in the PM series on the Westminster Larger Catechism and the Lord’s Supper. Covers WLC Questions 174-175 on the responsibilities of the minister in administering the supper and the duties of communicants after receiving it. Brings the series to a close with a summary of the catechism’s comprehensive teaching on the sacrament.
transcript:
## Receiving the Lord's Supper with Reverence

We are finishing our series using the Larger Catechism to deepen our understanding of the Lord's Supper. Tonight we consider questions 174 and 175, which address what we should do *during* the administration of the Supper and *afterward*.

## Question 174: What Is Required During the Lord's Supper?

The Larger Catechism answers that during the administration of the Lord's Supper, communicants are required to wait upon God with all holy reverence and attention, to carefully observe the sacramental elements and actions, to heedfully discern the Lord's body, and to affectionately meditate on his death and sufferings — and thereby stir up a vigorous exercise of their graces, to judge themselves and to sorrow for sin, and to hunger and thirst after Christ, feeding on him by faith, receiving of his fullness, trusting in his merits, rejoicing in his love, giving thanks for his grace, and renewing their covenant with God and love toward all the saints.

That is a lot of words, but it boils down to this: **focus on Christ**. Remember what the bread and the cup signify, and why you are doing this.

### Trust the Lord with Reverence and Attention

Paul warns against the opposite — coming together without reverence:

> *"When you come together in one place, it is not to eat the Lord's Supper. For in eating, each one takes his own supper ahead of others, and one is hungry and another is drunk."* (1 Corinthians 11:20–21)

> *"Whoever eats this bread or drinks this cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord."* (1 Corinthians 11:27)

The Lord's Supper is not a casual occasion for catching up. It is a solemn event in which we reverently proclaim something. As Paul writes:

> *"For as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death till He comes."* (1 Corinthians 11:26)

If we are not paying attention, we are not proclaiming what we should be proclaiming.

### Discern the Lord's Body

We must be thinking about what the sacrament says about Christ's person and work:

> *"He who eats and drinks in an unworthy manner eats and drinks judgment to himself, not discerning the Lord's body."* (1 Corinthians 11:29)

> *"The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ?"* (1 Corinthians 10:16)

There is enough time during the passing of the elements to consider these truths.

### Meditate on Christ's Suffering and Death

We benefit by meditating on what Christ endured. Luke records the words of institution:

> *"This is My body which is given for you; do this in remembrance of Me. This cup is the new covenant in My blood, which is shed for you."* (Luke 22:19–20)

### Judge Your Own Sins

> *"For if we would judge ourselves, we would not be judged. But when we are judged, we are chastened by the Lord, that we may not be condemned with the world."* (1 Corinthians 11:31–32)

You need not take a lengthy inventory. Even selecting one specific sin to bring before the Lord each week is a meaningful practice.

### Hunger and Thirst After Christ

> *"I am the bread of life. He who comes to Me shall never hunger, and he who believes in Me shall never thirst."* (John 6:35)

As Paul describes his own pursuit of Christ in Philippians 3:7–9, so we hunger after that bread and thirst after that cup — feeding on Christ by faith.

### Renew the Covenant

> *"They continued steadfastly in the apostles' doctrine and fellowship, in the breaking of bread, and in prayers. And the Lord added to the church daily those who were being saved."* (Acts 2:42, 47)

This is not an ordinary meal. Paul says, "Do you not have houses to eat and drink in?" (1 Corinthians 11:22). It is a covenantal meal in which we renew our love for God and for one another.

## Question 175: What Is Required After the Lord's Supper?

The Catechism answers that after receiving the Supper, Christians should seriously consider how they have behaved during the ordinance and with what fruit. If they find quickening and comfort, they should bless God for it and watch against relapse. If they find no present benefit, they should review their preparation, wait for the fruit in due time, and approach the next Supper with greater care and diligence.

The Lord's Supper is always a means of grace — if you are not receiving benefit, the question is whether the problem lies in your own heart. Consider your preparation and whether you are harboring unconfessed sin. Then wait. Do not give up on the means God has given.

## The Session's Position Paper

Our session prepared a statement on the Lord's Supper that summarizes the theology we have studied:

*"God in His goodness and grace gave to His church the sacrament of the Lord's Supper as a means by which He gives grace to His people. It is an occasion when the whole person is engaged in the worship of God, both body and soul, which includes a whole range of emotions."*

The Supper is a whole-person act. We use all our senses: touch, smell, taste, sight, and hearing. And a range of emotions should accompany it.

*"The Lord's Supper confirms to the believer his or her union with Christ. Eating the bread and drinking the cup signifies that we are part of the body of Christ, united to him by faith. It is a time when the whole body communes with its head — and not merely private worship between the Christian and Jesus."*

This is why we do not practice private communion, and why a family cannot simply observe the Lord's Supper at home. It is given to the gathered church.

*"The Lord's Supper is not a regular meal. Even at the institution of this sacrament, our Lord waited until the regular Passover meal was over before introducing the sign of the new covenant."*

*"The Lord's Supper is a proclamation of Christ's death until he comes. It includes self-examination, great joy — for by his stripes we are healed — confident expectation of his return, a sober reckoning with the darkness of our sin, mourning for sin, and overflowing gratitude that grace has overcome sin."*

We strive to implement these things each Lord's Day: reading the words of institution, giving instruction on how to receive the Supper worthily, passing the elements so that all partake together as one body, and closing with a hymn that declares the resurrection joy we have in Christ.

## Conclusion

The Lord's Supper has a time-release quality. We do not benefit from it only while taking it; we benefit as we reflect on it throughout the week. Let the elements we have received be an ongoing meditation on what Christ has done, who he is, and who we are in him.
